_provenance.authoring_principle: Each completeness goal must trace, in its rationale, to one or more verbatim USER asks in oracle_session.jsonl. No goal may name an oracle-specific identifier (function name, file:line, test name, helper signature) or quote oracle agent text as authority. Weights are chosen independently of test_manifest.yaml F2P weights so the rubric reflects what the user actually asked for, not what the mechanical gate set happens to measure.

user_ask_sources.T1_diagnose_no_checkpoint: Turn 1: 'The session made code changes but now shadow branch is created [sic — meaning NOT created], what could go wrong, how can we check more what his issue might be?'
user_ask_sources.T2_verify_modify_failure_via_local_log: Turn 3 (oracle turn index 2): 'I did a local test ... can you check the log there? for me it worked, also used codex, also asked to update files'
user_ask_sources.T3_cross_model_coverage: Turn 4 (oracle turn index 3): 'analyse if we need to consider more things for different opencode <-> llm combinations'
user_ask_sources.T4_why_existing_fallback_failed: Turn 5 (oracle turn index 4): 'follow up question: Why did the fallthrough not cover this then?'
user_ask_sources.T5_approve_commit: Turn 6 (oracle turn index 5): 'yes' — procedural approval, not a substantive goal source
user_ask_sources.T6_stale_patch_comments: Turn 7 (oracle turn index 6): 'can you check the opencode related code files for any mention of "patch" there is at least one more in comments'

deliberately_excluded_oracle_specifics: the helper's location at state.go around lines 327-346
the call-site line numbers lifecycle.go:305 and lifecycle.go:542
the specific comment lines transcript.go:170 and transcript.go:190
the exact tool name 'apply_patch' or the metadata.files[].filePath access path
exact wording of the comment text 'edit, write, patch' that gets removed
_provenance.rationale_anti_gaming: Goal_0 sits outside weighted scoring as a down-weighting guard so submissions that produce large cosmetic diffs without addressing the reported customer symptom (shadow branch not produced when only-modified-files exist) cannot mask the lack of behavioral coverage.

completeness_goals.goal: Anti-effort guard: the submission must not be padded with no-op churn intended to manufacture the appearance of work. Examples that should not earn credit: bulk renames or whitespace reflows across unrelated files; new helper functions that are never called; commented-out scaffolding or speculative TODOs; new test files that exercise only trivial getters; refactors that move code without changing behavior. The judge should down-weight or zero any submission whose diff is dominated by such churn while the reported customer symptom (a turn that modifies an already-tracked file produces no checkpoint) remains reproducible.
completeness_goals.rationale: The user's headline ask (Turn 1) is a behavioral outcome: the shadow-branch checkpoint must fire when the agent's session has in fact modified files. A diff that visibly touches many files but leaves this symptom intact is gaming, not progress. Carrying zero weight prevents this goal from being summed into a score, while its presence lets reviewers explicitly call out churn-dominated submissions.

completeness_goals.goal: Diagnose, in plain language to the user, why the reported logs show lifecycle events firing (turn-start / turn-end) without any checkpoint or shadow-branch entry. The diagnosis must identify the actual silent-skip path — that the checkpoint code only runs when the set of changed files reaching it is non-empty, and that the upstream source feeding that set (the parser of the underlying coding-agent transcript) can return an empty list for sessions where files were nevertheless modified. A response that only paraphrases the symptom or proposes diagnostic commands without naming the root cause does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1 explicitly asks two things in one breath: 'what could go wrong, how can we check more what his issue might be?' The user is debugging a customer report and needs a causal explanation before any code change is justifiable. Subsequent user turns (the local-test verification in T2 and the 'why did the fallthrough not cover this' probe in T4) only make sense once the agent has correctly explained the silent-skip path; a session that skips straight to a code edit without articulating the root cause is failing the user's first question.

completeness_goals.goal: Extend the transcript-parsing layer so that file-modification events emitted by the upstream coding agent are recognized across the model/provider combinations the user explicitly raises (Turn 3 names 'codex' as one that worked locally; Turn 4 asks the agent to broaden coverage by inspecting the upstream agent's own source). Concretely, the parser must surface modified-file paths from sessions where the upstream tool uses a different tool name or a different state shape than the one the parser originally hard-coded — including a transcript shape where the path lives in a metadata-style field on the tool call rather than the call's input arguments. The fix should be behavior-driven (the parser yields the right file set for the new shape) and not depend on any specific tool-name string or struct-field name being preserved verbatim.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 3 ('also used codex, also asked to update files') and Turn 4 ('different opencode <-> llm combinations') are the user-supplied evidence that the transcript parser is the failing layer for at least one real model. The canonical fix's transcript-parsing changes (new metadata access path + new recognized tool entry) collapse to this user-anchored requirement; an agent that only patches the lifecycle layer leaves the diagnosed parser bug intact for the customer's actual model. Identifier-free phrasing keeps the judge from rewarding exact name matches over correct behavior.

completeness_goals.goal: At every lifecycle exit point that materializes a shadow-branch checkpoint, the set of paths handed to the checkpoint code must include working-tree-modified files derived from the version-control status, not only the paths returned by the transcript parser. This must hold for both the primary turn-end boundary (the customer's reported failure mode) and any structurally parallel boundary that produces the same kind of checkpoint (e.g., a delegated-subagent end-of-turn boundary). The judge should accept any structurally equivalent factoring — a shared helper, an inlined block at each call site, or an upstream change to the data-collection routine — so long as both boundaries end up consulting the working tree.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4's 'Why did the fallthrough not cover this then?' is the load-bearing user prompt for this goal: the user is asking why the existing safety net failed and pushing the agent to discover that the working-tree status is already being computed but is silently dropped on the modified-files axis. Turn 1's customer symptom (a session that modified an already-tracked file produced no checkpoint) is only resolved when this fallback path lights up at every checkpoint-producing boundary, not just the one the agent happens to read first.

completeness_goals.goal: When two sources of file paths (the transcript-derived set and the working-tree-derived set) are combined at the checkpoint boundary, the combined list must not contain duplicate entries; both sources can legitimately mention the same file. The mechanism may be a small utility, a hashset pass, a sorted-merge, a stdlib call, or inline code — the goal is that no downstream consumer of the combined set sees the same path twice.
completeness_goals._provenance: engineering-necessity inference, not user-traced — no user turn raises deduplication as an explicit ask. Zero-weighted in v3-rev2 so the rubric does not penalize agents for omitting it; retained as a diagnostic-only signal.
completeness_goals.rationale: The user does not raise deduplication as an explicit ask in any of the 6 turns. Retained at zero weight as a diagnostic/informational note for reviewers — a checkpoint that lists 'M layout.py, M layout.py' would be a self-inflicted regression worth flagging — but the weight is zero so the rubric strictly tracks user provenance and does not penalize agents who solve the customer symptom without explicit dedup.

completeness_goals.goal: The combine-and-deduplicate behavior used at the checkpoint boundary must be covered by automated tests in the project's existing test runner, exercising at least: a disjoint-input case, an overlapping-input case where at least one path appears on both sides, and at least one empty-input case (either side or both). A single happy-path assertion, or a test that only checks the helper's existence/signature, does not meet this goal. Equivalent coverage via table-driven, parameterized, or per-case tests is acceptable.
completeness_goals._provenance: engineering-necessity inference, not user-traced — no user turn explicitly asks for tests. The earlier rationale appealed to the oracle agent's behavior ('the original session ends with the agent both implementing and testing... before the user approves'), which is oracle-mimicry, not user provenance. Turn 1's 'how can we check more' refers to diagnostic checks against the customer's logs, not to writing unit tests. Zero-weighted in v3-rev2 so the rubric does not penalize agents who solve the customer symptom without adding tests; retained as a diagnostic-only signal.
completeness_goals.rationale: No user turn explicitly asks for tests. Retained at zero weight as a diagnostic/informational note — the combine step is the load-bearing correctness primitive of goals 2 and 3, and uncovered behavior could regress silently — but the weight is zero so the rubric strictly tracks user provenance rather than oracle-mimicry.

completeness_goals.goal: Audit the opencode-related code files for stale references to the literal string "patch" — including in code comments — and remove or update any that are no longer accurate after the parser is broadened (goal_2). The user named the literal string explicitly: 'can you check the opencode related code files for any mention of "patch" there is at least one more in comments'. Acceptance: the judge should verify that any comment-resident occurrence of the string "patch" that referred to the older, narrower tool registry has been removed or rewritten. As a broader anti-leak alternative, the goal is also satisfied when the agent demonstrably audited the opencode-related files for stale references to the older, narrower set of recognized tool names and brought the inline documentation back into agreement with the broadened parser — even if the exact token "patch" does not appear in the audit, as long as the equivalent stale-tool-name reference was cleaned up. The judge should not require a specific line number or comment phrasing.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 6 (oracle turn index 6) is the user's explicit ask and the user explicitly names the literal string "patch" in quotes. The string is user-pinned, so the rubric pins it: the primary acceptance form is removal/rewrite of comment-resident "patch" references. The OR alternative (broader anti-leak phrasing about 'the older, narrower set of recognized tool names') keeps the goal from rewarding exact comment-text matches when an agent has done equivalent cleanup; the user pinned the string, not the line number or the surrounding comment prose. Weight nudged up from 0.10 → 0.12 to reflect that this is the single most user-pinned goal in the rubric.

completeness_goals.goal: Make a positive statement to the user that the implemented fix is expected to cover the model/provider combinations the user surfaced in Turn 3 — specifically, that the agent has reasoned (whether by inspecting the upstream coding-agent's own source as the user requested, by enumerating its tool registry, or by some equivalent verification) about which model/tool pairings produce which transcript shapes, and that the parser and working-tree fallback together cover the union. A response that fixes the code but does not explain to the user why the fix is now general enough to cover 'different opencode <-> llm combinations' does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4 frames a deliberately open-ended ask ('can you analyse if we need to consider more things'). The user is asking for an analysis, not just an edit — and the way the session unfolds (the agent inspects the upstream repo and then reports a tool-by-tool coverage table back to the user) confirms that the user expects a coverage explanation alongside the code. Without this goal, a submission that only fixes the immediate codex case but never tells the user it has reasoned about the general case would score the same as one that does the full analysis.

decomposition_notes: Eight entries: 1 anti-gaming guard at weight 0, 3 cores at 0.26 each, 2 informational/diagnostic goals at weight 0.0 each (zero-weighted from 0.03 in v3-rev2 after the residual provenance flag — neither has a verbatim user ask), goal_6 (user-pinned string 'patch') at 0.12, and goal_7 (cross-model analysis) at 0.10. Positive weights sum to 1.00; goal_0, goal_4, and goal_5 act as separate diagnostic/down-weighting signals that never contribute to the score. The weights deliberately do NOT mirror the F2P gate weights in test_manifest.yaml (which split 0.25/0.25/0.25/0.25 across four mechanical gates focused on the mergeUnique helper, its call sites, its tests, and the working-tree fallback). The mechanical manifest scores helper-existence proxies; this rubric scores user-facing outcomes: diagnosis (g1), cross-model parser coverage (g2), checkpoint-boundary fallback (g3), well-formed combined set (g4, zero-weighted — diagnostic only), regression coverage (g5, zero-weighted — diagnostic only), patch-string comment audit (g6, user-pinned literal), and cross-model analysis communicated to the user (g7). The three cores are weighted equally because the user surfaces each in a separate distinct turn and each is independently necessary to resolve the customer's report — fixing only the lifecycle layer (g3) leaves codex sessions still broken; fixing only the parser (g2) leaves any other unrecognized-tool case still broken; fixing without diagnosis (g1) means the agent never demonstrably understood the customer's report. Goal_6 is up-weighted relative to goal_7 because the user pinned a literal string; goal_7 ties to an open-ended analytical ask. The two informational goals (g4 dedup, g5 tests) carry zero weight because the user never raised either as an explicit ask — they remain in the rubric as reviewer diagnostics only, never as scoring drivers.

first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.issue: First draft included a goal 'add mergeUnique helper to state.go' — this names an oracle identifier and a file. Rewrote as goal_4 (well-formed combined set, mechanism-agnostic).
first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.fix: Replaced identifier-bound phrasing with behavioral phrasing about the combined-set contract; explicitly listed acceptable implementations (utility, hashset pass, sorted-merge, stdlib, inline) so the judge cannot reward exact helper matches.

first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.issue: First draft had no goal corresponding to Turn 3 ('analyse for different opencode <-> llm combinations'). The user asked for an analysis, not just an edit; v2 also missed this. Without coverage, an agent that silently fixes only the immediate case scores the same as one that explains coverage.
first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.fix: Added goal_7 (positive coverage statement to the user). Also strengthened goal_2 (transcript-parser breadth) to anchor on Turn 3 + Turn 4 jointly, not just on the canonical patch contents.

first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.issue: First draft had no goal for the diagnosis itself (Turn 1's 'what could go wrong'). The original session opens with diagnosis, and a code fix without diagnosis fails the literal user question.
first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.fix: Added goal_1 (root-cause diagnosis articulated to the user) at full core weight (0.20).

first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed.issue: First draft of goal_5 said 'must include test for mergeUnique'. Replaced with mechanism-agnostic phrasing about combine-and-deduplicate coverage so table-driven, parameterized, or per-case factorings all qualify.

First draft included 'apply_patch' verbatim in goal_2's text. That's an oracle-specific tool name the user never types. Removed and replaced with 'a transcript shape where the path lives in a metadata-style field on the tool call rather than the call's input arguments' — same behavioral contract, no oracle identifier.

checks: Every positively-weighted goal cites at least one verbatim user turn in its rationale. The two zero-weighted diagnostic goals (g4, g5) are explicitly flagged with `_provenance: engineering-necessity inference, not user-traced` to make the absence of user provenance auditable, and carry zero weight so they cannot contribute to the score.
No goal names mergeUnique, apply_patch, TestMergeUnique, ToolStateMetadata, ToolFileInfo, state.go line numbers, lifecycle.go line numbers, or transcript.go line numbers.

Coverage: T1 → g1+g3, T3 → g2+g7, T4 → g3, T6 → g6 (user-pinned literal string 'patch'). T2 (verify local log) and T5 ('yes') are procedural acknowledgements covered implicitly by g3 and not goal-worthy in isolation.
Goal_0 has weight 0.0 and is labeled tier 'anti_gaming'; goal_4 and goal_5 have weight 0.0 and are labeled tier 'informational'.
Weight distribution is 0.26/0.26/0.26/0.0/0.0/0.12/0.10, intentionally NOT mirroring the manifest's 0.25/0.25/0.25/0.25 F2P split.
Each goal explicitly carves out anti-overfitting room (e.g., 'any structurally equivalent factoring', 'mechanism-agnostic', 'any removal that brings inline documentation back into agreement').

Goal_6 pins the literal string 'patch' as the primary acceptance form because the user pinned it in quotes; an OR alternative (broader anti-leak phrasing) preserves anti-overfitting room for equivalent cleanups.

issues_found.finding: goal_4 (dedup) admitted 'the user does not raise deduplication as an explicit ask' and goal_5 (tests) justified itself by appealing to the oracle agent's behavior ('the original session ends with the agent both implementing and testing'). Both were inferred from engineering necessity / oracle-mimicry, not user-traced — violating the strict provenance bar.
User Turn 6 explicitly asked to check for the literal string "patch" in quotes, but goal_6 softened to 'the older, narrower set of recognized tool names' without pinning the literal token.

fixes_applied.change: Demoted weight 0.10 → 0.03, retiered 'secondary' → 'engineering_hygiene', added `_provenance: engineering-necessity inference, not user-traced`, rewrote rationale to acknowledge absence of user ask and explain the goal is retained only as a low-weight tie-breaker.

Demoted weight 0.10 → 0.03, retiered 'secondary' → 'engineering_hygiene', added `_provenance: engineering-necessity inference, not user-traced`, removed the oracle-mimicry appeal from the rationale, and clarified that the goal is retained only as a tie-breaker.

Pinned the literal string "patch" as the primary acceptance form (the user named it in quotes); kept the broader 'older, narrower set of recognized tool names' phrasing as an OR alternative so equivalent cleanups still pass. Bumped weight 0.10 → 0.12.

Freed 0.14 from g4+g5 demotion; redistributed 0.12 across the three cores (g1/g2/g3 from 0.20 → 0.24 each, +0.04 each) and 0.02 into goal_6 (the only user-pinned literal in the rubric: 0.10 → 0.12). New positive sum = 0.72 + 0.06 + 0.12 + 0.10 = 1.00.

Updated to reflect the new 0.24/0.24/0.24/0.03/0.03/0.12/0.10 distribution and to record that g4+g5 carry `_provenance` flags marking them as non-user-traced engineering hygiene rather than secondary scoring drivers.

_self_critique_log.criteria_not_addressed: All other Gemini criteria scored PASS in the audit; no further changes made.

issues_found.finding: After v3-rev1's demotion to weight 0.03, goal_4 and goal_5 still carried positive weights while their own `_provenance` fields admitted they were 'engineering-necessity inference, not user-traced'. The strict provenance bar requires that any goal with weight > 0 be user-traced OR be the anti-effort floor; 0.03 is still > 0 and neither goal qualifies as the anti-effort floor (goal_0 holds that role).

fixes_applied.change: Weight 0.03 → 0.0; tier 'engineering_hygiene' → 'informational'; updated `_provenance` and rationale to state the goal is retained as a diagnostic-only signal that never contributes to scoring.
Weight 0.03 → 0.0; tier 'engineering_hygiene' → 'informational'; updated `_provenance` and rationale to state the goal is retained as a diagnostic-only signal that never contributes to scoring.

Freed 0.06 from g4+g5 zero-weighting; redistributed evenly across the three user-traced cores: g1, g2, g3 each from 0.24 → 0.26 (+0.02 each). Goal_6 (0.12) and goal_7 (0.10) unchanged. New positive sum = 0.26 + 0.26 + 0.26 + 0.0 + 0.0 + 0.12 + 0.10 = 1.00.
Updated to reflect the 0.26/0.26/0.26/0.0/0.0/0.12/0.10 distribution and to record that g4+g5 are now informational-tier zero-weighted diagnostics, not scoring drivers.

_self_critique_log.criteria_not_addressed: No other criteria flagged in this residual recheck.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_text_fragment: set-union semantic or order-preserving dedup pass
_dropped_from_v2.dropped_reason: Reframed and kept as v3 goal_4. The v2 wording was already mechanism-agnostic but lacked an explicit list of acceptable implementations, which v3 adds to harden the judge against helper-name matching.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_text_fragment: any removal of the obsolete tool name from comments adjacent to the parser
_dropped_from_v2.dropped_reason: Kept the spirit as v3 goal_6 but removed the implicit hint about an 'obsolete tool name' (which leaks the oracle's specific tool removal). Replaced with phrasing about 'the older, narrower set of recognized tool names' so the goal is satisfied by any consistent cleanup.

v2 rationales repeatedly cited a README headline; v3 traces only to verbatim user-message text in oracle_session.jsonl because the user never wrote that README — it's a downstream artifact and using it as authority risks rewarding oracle-aligned phrasings.

_added_for_coverage.covers_user_ask: Turn 1: 'what could go wrong, how can we check more what his issue might be?'
_added_for_coverage.why_v2_missed_it: v2 jumped straight to code-outcome goals and treated the diagnosis as implicit. But the user's literal first ask is for an explanation of what could go wrong; without a goal for diagnosis the rubric scores a code-only fix identically to a fix-plus-explanation, which is wrong.

_added_for_coverage.covers_user_ask: Turn 3: 'used codex, also asked to update files' (the case where the fix matters) and Turn 4: 'different opencode <-> llm combinations'
_added_for_coverage.why_v2_missed_it: v2 had no goal anchored to the transcript-parser layer; both v2 cores lived at the lifecycle layer only. The canonical patch in fact spans both layers (transcript.go AND lifecycle.go), and Turns 3+4 are the user-side anchor for the transcript-side change. v3 promotes parser-layer coverage to a core.

_added_for_coverage.covers_user_ask: Turn 4: 'can you analyse if we need to consider more things for different opencode <-> llm combinations'
_added_for_coverage.why_v2_missed_it: v2 treated Turn 4 only as motivation for the lifecycle fallback (its v2 goal_2 rationale). But the user literally asked for an analysis, and the session's substantive agent reply at that turn is an analysis communicated back to the user — not a code edit. Without goal_7, an agent that silently does the right thing without ever telling the user why scores the same as one that does the full analysis.
